acholuria: A jaundiced patient produces pale, straw-colored urine instead of the tea-brown one expects. What the urine lacks matters more than what it holds.
anuria: Past oliguria. The kidneys have quit.
bordetella: Named for the Belgian who first cultured it. Its most famous product is a sound.
bronchiectasis: The 'signet ring' sign on a CT slice — an airway wider than the vessel beside it — traces back to a childhood insult that never quite healed.
burns: Graded by depth, estimated by nines.
cachexia: The gaunt appearance of late-stage disease that calories cannot undo.
catatonia: The patient will hold whatever pose the examiner leaves them in. First described on the nineteenth-century psychiatric ward, and increasingly recognized in autoimmune encephalitis.
cheilosis: The Greek stem is the word for lip.
choledocholithiasis: A stone lodges where two systems share plumbing. The result: pain on the right, dark on the way in, pale on the way out.
cholecystectomy: The Greek roots read as 'cutting out the bile bladder.' Four small ports and a scope in modern hands.
choroiditis: Floaters and a slowly blurring vision. The inflammation is deeper than the retina — in the vascular carpet behind it.
colitis: The endoscope crosses the ileocecal valve and finds an angry, weeping mucosa. The suffix names the finding, not the cause.
crigler: A rare hereditary jaundice named for two mid-20th-century American pediatricians. The liver cannot conjugate what needs conjugating.
dacryoadenitis: A tender swelling above the outer corner of the eye.
dislocation: A shoulder pops out of its socket after an outstretched-arm fall — the joint no longer articulates.
dissociation: A trauma survivor watches themselves from outside the body. The psychiatric umbrella term is a single noun.
dupuytrens: A palm slowly refuses to open. Named for a Napoleonic-era French surgeon; particularly common in men of Northern European descent.
dysgeusia: Metallic, muted, or wrong. A distortion of one of the classical five.
dysmenorrhea: Prostaglandins peak in the first two days. NSAIDs quiet them.
dysuria: The burning that sends her to the dipstick.
ecchymosis: The evolving color chart of a bruise, in one Greek-rooted word.
ecthyma: A deeper, ulcerative, punched-out form of a superficial bacterial skin infection. The plate looks worse than a routine impetigo lesion.
epiglottitis: A child sits forward, drools, and does not want to be moved. Airway first, imaging never.
erysipelas: A bright fiery patch of skin whose edge you could trace with a coastline. Group A streptococci; the classical name predates germ theory.
erythema: The Greek stem for redness. Hides inside the names of many rashes.
euvolemia: The Goldilocks state of intravascular fluid.
fibrosis: What replaces working tissue when the body gives up on healing.
fungemia: A yeast grows out of a blood culture drawn from an ICU line. The physician's face changes.
gliadin: A protein fraction of wheat that certain small bowels cannot forgive.
hordeolum: The Latin for barley. A warm compress and time.
hyperaldosteronism: Resistant hypertension in an otherwise-healthy 40-year-old, plus a stubbornly low potassium. A hormone is overshooting.
kwashiorkor: A Ghanaian word: 'the sickness the older child gets when the next is born.'
laryngomalacia: Noisy inspiratory breathing in a newborn that quiets by the second birthday as the cartilage stiffens.
lassa: A Nigerian town. A rodent. An arenavirus. 1969.
lymphedema: Months after the axillary nodes were dissected, the arm still swells and never quite comes back down.
molluscum: Little pearls with a dimple in the center. Poxvirus; children swap them at the pool.
mucocele: A blue-tinged blister on the inside of a lower lip after a minor bite.
muscular: The adjective most often paired with 'dystrophy.'
nosocomial: You caught it because of where you were, not what you did.
oligodendroglioma: A middle-aged accountant's first seizure. Imaging finds a slow-growing frontal mass flecked with calcium.
orthopnea: The number of pillows a patient sleeps propped on is a bedside cardiac exam.
parkinsonism: Named for a nineteenth-century London physician who described a 'shaking palsy.'
peliosis: A bodybuilder on stanozolol. An AIDS patient with Bartonella. Both grow small blood-filled cavities where healthy tissue should be.
periodontitis: The teeth are still there; what anchors them is not.
petechiae: A glass tumbler pressed to a feverish child's chest. What does not blanch is counted in single millimeters.
phthisis: The Greek-rooted name from the sanatorium era, a full century before the bacillus was identified.
plague: Rodents, fleas, buboes, and a nursery rhyme.
polyneuritis: A symmetric burning-and-tingling of the feet that climbs the legs, worse at night.
polyneuropathy: The stocking-and-glove numbness that ascends over years. Diabetes leads the differential.
pyoderma: A pus-producing skin infection in general — but the word also names a striking ulcerative autoimmune disorder that responds to steroids, not antibiotics.
rickets: An English name for what happened to the bones of nineteenth-century factory children who never saw the sun.
sarcopenia: The slow disappearance of what you can neither borrow nor buy.
sialadenitis: The salivary reflex at the smell of lemon suddenly hurts.
strangulated: The adjective that turns a trapped hernia from urgent to emergent.
takayasu: A Japanese ophthalmologist's name attaches to an arteritis of young women, sometimes producing pulseless arms.
thromboembolism: A clot forms in one place, breaks free, and lodges in another. One compound word for the whole cascade.
tracheomalacia: A newborn with a barking cough and stridor that worsens with crying. The cartilaginous rings are too soft.
ureteric: An adjective. Often carries 'stone' or 'reflux' behind it.
vaginitis: Bacterial, candidal, or trichomonal — the noun for the inflamed organ is the same for all three.
varicose: Cashiers know them. Flight attendants know them. Ropes on the calves that tell a story about gravity and a failing set of valves.
volvulus: A coffee bean on an abdominal film.
vulvovaginitis: A prepubertal girl and a postmenopausal woman share a complaint. Without estrogen to keep the mucosa in trim, the flora shifts and the tissue reacts.
whipple: An American surgeon lent his name to a pancreatic-head resection — and, separately, to a bacterial malabsorption disease he found at autopsy.
whooping: A characteristic inspiratory sound follows the paroxysms of dry cough. The vaccine that prevents it is the P in DTaP.
